10: A direct revelation: 'They shall look on me, on him whom they have pierced, and they shall mourn for him, as one mourns for an only child, and weep bitterly over him, as one weeps over a firstborn.' The shift from 'me' (YHWH speaking) to 'him' (the one pierced) within the verse is the OT's most striking grammatical prolepsis of the incarnation: the one pierced is YHWH, yet YHWH speaks of him in the third person. John cites it at the crucifixion (Zechariah 19:37: these things took place that the Scripture might be fulfilled: they will look on him whom they have pierced), and Revelation applies it to the parousia (Zechariah 1:7: every eye will see him, even those who pierced him, and all tribes of the earth will wail on account of him). The mourning is both repentance (Acts 2:37: they were cut to the heart and said, Brothers, what shall we do?) and eschatological recognition (Revelation 1:7: all tribes will wail).
1: The second massa’ (oracle/burden) introduces the eschatological diptych of Zechariah 12-14. The triple creation-formula (“who stretched out the heavens, laid the foundation of the earth, formed the human spirit within”) grounds what follows in YHWH’s cosmic sovereignty. The creator-God who made heaven, earth, and human spirit is fully competent to act in human history. John 1:3 (“all things were made through him”) identifies the Logos-Christ with the creation act invoked here, authorizing his eschatological role.
2: Jerusalem as a cup of reeling (saf ra’al) for surrounding nations — those who drink from it stagger. The eschatological siege is a cup that intoxicates the besiegers. Jeremiah’s cup-of-wrath imagery (Jeremiah 25:15-28) and Revelation 16:19 (“Babylon was given the cup of the wine of God’s fury”) share this idiom. Jesus in Gethsemane prays about the “cup” (Matthew 26:39) — he who drinks the cup of wrath himself so that Jerusalem need not be the staggering-cup for the world.
3: Jerusalem as a “heavy stone” (even ma’amasāh) that injures all who try to lift it. The image shifts from cup to stone: those who attempt to move Jerusalem against divine will herniate themselves. This stone-that-injures motif links to the cornerstone of Psalms 118:22 and Zechariah 10:4: the stone that injures those who handle it wrongly is the same stone that is the foundation for those who build on it correctly. 1 Peter 2:7-8 holds both together: for believers, the stone is precious; for unbelievers, “a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense.”
4: YHWH will strike the attacking nations’ horses with panic and their riders with madness, while watching over Judah. The divine targeting inverts the normal military advantage of cavalry. The phrase “I will watch over the house of Judah” (ve’al beit yehudāh epqach einay) echoes the promise of Zechariah 9:8 (YHWH watching with his own eyes). The protecting gaze of God is a theme throughout the Psalms (Psalms 121:4: “he who keeps Israel will neither slumber nor sleep”). Christ as the one who watches over his church (Matthew 28:20: “I am with you always”) is the incarnate form of this promise.
5: The clans of Judah acknowledging that Jerusalem’s inhabitants “draw their strength from the LORD of Hosts, their God” — a confession of source. This is not self-confidence but theocentric dependence. The specific formula “their strength from YHWH of Hosts” anticipates the NT call to “be strong in the Lord and in the strength of his might” (Ephesians 6:10). Zechariah’s clans discover what Paul declares: human weakness is the venue for divine power (2 Corinthians 12:9-10).
6: The clans of Judah like a fire pot among logs, a flaming torch among sheaves — consuming the surrounding peoples. Fire as divine weapon in the hands of YHWH’s people echoes Elijah’s fire (1 Kgs 18) and anticipates the Pentecostal fire (Acts 2:3: tongues of fire). The gospel as consuming fire (Hebrews 12:29: “our God is a consuming fire”) spreads through proclamation. Jerusalem “remains inhabited in its own place” — the city endures while the surrounding powers are consumed.
7: YHWH saves the settlements of Judah first, lest the prestige of Jerusalem overshadow them. The deliberate reversal of priority (periphery before center) is a divine equity principle. Jesus embodies this principle: he ministers first to the margins (Galilee before Jerusalem, Samaritans, tax collectors, lepers) and enters Jerusalem last in his ministry. The last-first inversion of Matthew 20:16 and the movement of Paul’s mission from “Jerusalem... to the end of the earth” (Acts 1:8) both participate in this divine logic of reversal.
8: “The weakest among them will be like David, and the house of David like God, like the angel of the LORD.” The eschatological elevation is exponential: the weak become David-strong; the Davidic house becomes God-like/angel-like. The phrase ke-elohim ke-mal’akh YHWH lifneihem (like God, like the angel of the LORD before them) echoes the tabernacle/conquest period when the angel of the LORD led Israel (Exodus 23:20-23). Christ is simultaneously the weak one (Philippians 2:7-8: taking the form of a servant) and the one at whose name every knee bows (Philippians 2:10-11).
9: The divine commitment: “I will set out to destroy all the nations that attack Jerusalem.” Biqqeshtî (I will seek to destroy) — the language of determined divine pursuit. The reversal of the nations’ initiative: they come to attack; YHWH takes the offensive. Revelation 19:11-21 depicts Christ returning on a white horse to defeat the nations assembled against the holy city. The promise of Zechariah 12:9 is fulfilled in apocalyptic scope at the parousia.
11: The mourning in Jerusalem will be “as intense as the mourning for Hadad-rimmon in the plain of Megiddo.” Hadad-rimmon is probably a place near Megiddo (or possibly a divine name invoked at a mourning rite). The mourning at Megiddo most likely refers to the lament over Josiah’s death (2 Chronicles 35:24-25 records national mourning; Jeremiah 22:10 alludes to it). Josiah, the greatest reforming king, cut down at the height of his reform — the paradigmatic grief for unjust loss of a righteous leader. The mourning of Zechariah 12:10 (for the pierced one) is like grief for the righteous king prematurely cut off: a template for Passion mourning.
12: The mourning is organized by family divisions, husband and wife separately, each lineage privately. The enumerated families (Davidic, Nathanic, Levitic, Shimeite) cover royal, prophetic, priestly, and administrative streams. The breadth of the mourning (every family; every lineage) signals total societal grief. The account of the burial of Jesus involves women mourning (Luke 23:27-28) and the disciples scattered; John 19:25-27 has a small group at the cross. The universal mourning Zechariah envisions is the eschatological scope of what Calvary initiates.
13: The Levitical family and the Shimeite family mourn separately, with the women separately in each case. The Levites are the priestly tribe; Shimei is a subclan of Levi (Numbers 3:18). The specific enumeration of the priestly tribe as mourning is striking: the very lineage responsible for the temple cult mourns the pierced one. In the Passion account, after Jesus’ death “the curtain of the temple was torn in two” (Matthew 27:51) — the priests’ world is upended. 1 Peter 2:9 reconstitutes the mourning priestly family as a “royal priesthood.”
14: All remaining families, each privately, with women separately. The totality phrase closes the mourning list: every lineage is implicated. No family is exempt from the grief. This comprehensive mourning reverses the sin of corporate indifference: the flock that was scattered (Zechariah 11:16-17) is now gathered in grief. Interestingly, the separation of men and women for mourning in the text may reflect both ritual propriety and the intensity of individual grief. The resurrection appearances — often first to women (Matthew 28:1-10; John 20:11-18) — turn this grief to joy in a way that involves the women first.
